title: Replacing a shower cartridge
subtitle: Swap a worn thermostatic or pressure-balance cartridge to restore correct temperature control
excerpt: How to replace the cartridge in a thermostatic shower valve: isolating both hot and cold supplies, removing the handle and escutcheon, extracting the old cartridge, fitting an exact-match replacement, and testing temperature control.

content.text: A worn thermostatic cartridge is the most common cause of a shower that no longer holds temperature, or that drips when the valve is shut. Before ordering a replacement, find the valve maker and model. The maker and model are usually stamped on the valve body inside the wall. Order the exact-match cartridge for that valve. There is no universal cartridge that fits every valve.
Step 1, isolate both hot and cold supplies

Locate the service valves for both the hot and cold supplies to the shower. These are usually accessible via a panel in the wall adjacent to the shower, or under the bath. Close both valves (quarter-turn so the slot is perpendicular to the pipe). Open the shower briefly to confirm both supplies are isolated and to drain residual pressure from the valve. Do not proceed with water still present in the valve.
Step 2, remove the handle and escutcheon

Locate and remove the handle screw, usually hidden under a decorative cap at the centre of the handle. Pull the handle off the valve stem (it may need a gentle wiggle). Unscrew or unclip the escutcheon plate to expose the valve body. The valve body is usually fixed to the wall with two or four screws under the escutcheon; do not disturb these unless replacing the valve body itself.

using long-nose pliers or a screwdriver tip to push the legs inward before pulling the clip out. Grip the cartridge body with water-pump pliers and pull directly outward. The cartridge slides out of the valve body; it should not need more than moderate hand pressure. If it is seized, soak with white vinegar for 30 minutes before pulling.

Note the orientation of the old cartridge before removing it (mark the top with tape). Insert the new cartridge in the same orientation. Many cartridges have a flat, keyway, or tab that fixes them in one orientation only; do not force it in the wrong way. Press the cartridge fully into the valve body until it is flush. Refit the retaining clip, pressing the legs outward until they engage the clip groove.

Refit the escutcheon and handle without fully tightening. Open both service valves slowly. Turn on the shower and run through the full temperature range: the temperature should track smoothly from cold to hot without dead zones. Check for any drips at the cartridge face. If temperature control is correct, tighten the handle and escutcheon.

attrs.fix: Isolate, extract the cartridge, rotate it 180 degrees, refit the retaining clip, and test again.
attrs.cause: The new cartridge was inserted 180 degrees out of orientation, reversing the hot and cold ports.
attrs.symptom: Shower runs only hot or only cold after replacing the cartridge

attrs.fix: Isolate, remove the escutcheon, and press the retaining clip fully into its groove. The clip legs must engage the housing groove on both sides.
attrs.cause: The retaining clip is not fully engaged, allowing the cartridge to ride back slightly under pressure.
attrs.symptom: Water drips from the valve face when the shower is off

attrs.fix: Confirm the cartridge part number against the valve manufacturer's specification. If pressures are unbalanced (common on combi boilers), fit a pressure-reducing valve on the cold supply to the shower.
attrs.cause: The replacement cartridge is not an exact match for the valve, or the supply pressures are significantly unbalanced.
attrs.symptom: Temperature fluctuates even with the new cartridge
